1.description: Currency investment strategies Panel A shows plain portfolio average excess returns (вЃ XВЇ(%)вЃ , in percent annualized) and standard deviations (вЃ ПѓПѓX(%)вЃ , in percent annualized), proxying for risk. Panel B plots the cumulative monthly returns of HML portfolios in percent. We consider nine investment strategies: carry (Carry), short-term momentum (Mom (ST)), long-term momentum (Mom (LT)), net foreign assets (NFA), liabilities in domestic currency (LDC), value (Value), term spreads (Term), long-term bond yields (LYld), and output gap (GAP). See Internet Appendix (Section II) for a detailed description of the strategies. The sample spans the November 1983 to December 2017 period at a monthly frequency (T = 410).

2.description: Largest normalized eigenvalues The figure shows the largest normalized eigenvalues of the matrix П‰П‰ОЈRP(П‰)=1TXвЉ¤X+П‰XВЇвЉ¤XВЇвЃ , for different values of the RP-weight (П‰). The T Г— N matrix X collects currency portfolio excess returns from the nine investment strategies (i.e., N = 46), and XВЇ denote their sample averages. The eigenvalues are normalized by the average idiosyncratic variance (вЃ ПµПѓВЇПµ2вЃ ) and hence relate more closely to factorsвЂ™ signal-to-noise ratios, being informative about factorsвЂ™ вЂњsignal strengths.вЂќ Specifically, ПµПѓПµПѓВЇПµ2=1Nв€‘n=1NПѓПµ,n2вЃ , where ПѓПµПѓПµ,n2 are the variances of the residuals obtained by estimating N time-series regressions, О±П‰П€ПµXnt=О±n+F^t(П‰)П€nвЉ¤+Пµnt,вЂ‰n=1вЂ¦,N test assets, t=1вЂ¦,T months, where П‰F^t(П‰) stacks the latent factors associated with the six largest eigenvalues of matrix П‰ОЈRP(П‰)вЃ , and thus vary with the RP-weight. Panel A reports the normalized eigenvalues, П‰ОЅП‰ПѓПµП‰ОЅЛњk(П‰)=ОЅk(П‰)/ПѓПµ2(П‰)вЃ . Panel B presents the difference of consecutive normalized eigenvalues, П‰П‰ОЅЛњk(П‰)в€’ОЅЛњk+1(П‰)вЃ , for k=2,вЂ¦,5вЃ . Panel C shows the eigenvalues scaled by the corresponding PCA (вЃ П‰П‰=в€’1вЃ ) eigenvalues, П‰П‰ОЅЛњk(П‰)/ОЅЛњk(П‰=в€’1)вЃ . In the legend, for a given RP-weight we present the optimal number of latent factors entering the SDF according to the Onatski (2010), O(#), Giglio and Xiu (2021), and GX(#) tests. The GX test is implemented with medium overfitting penalty value 0.5.

3.description: HML portfolio risk exposures to latent factors The figure shows HML portfolio loadings on the six estimated latent factors (left chart) and the associated R2s obtained by regressing the HML portfolio returns on the latent factors (right chart). The test assetsвЂ™ sample consists of the portfolios associated with the nine investment strategies (N = 46), thus HML portfolios are excluded from the estimation of latent factors (see Section II in the Internet Appendix for a description of the investment strategies). Hence, we infer HML portfolio loadings ex post from the corner portfolio loadings of the associated strategy, as the difference between P5/6 (high) and P1 (low) loadings, obtained by means of RP-PCA with baseline penalty value, that is, П‰ = 20. We obtain the HML portfoliosвЂ™ explained variations by estimating 9 Г— 6 OLS time-series regressions of the type of Equation (7), that is, О±П€ПµXnt=О±n+F^tП€nвЉ¤+ПµntвЃ , for n=1,вЂ¦,NвЃ , and t=1,вЂ¦,TвЃ . For a given HML portfolio, we run a total of 6 regressions as we include factors one by one. In this way, we can determine the marginal R2 contributions of each factor. The numbers above the bars refer to F^1tвЂ™s contributions. We present the N Г— K individual portfolio risk exposures and R2s in Figures A1 and A2 in the Internet Appendix. The sample spans the November 1983 to December 2017 period at a monthly frequency (T = 410).

4.description: Observed versus model-implied portfolio excess returns The figure shows the realized average excess returns (Observed XВЇвЃ , in percent annualized) and the estimated, model-implied expected excess returns (Model XВЇвЃ , in percent annualized) of the 46 currency portfolios (test assets) associated with the nine currency investment strategies, described in Section II in the Internet Appendix. For a given portfolio n, the model-implied expected excess returns are the fitted values from the second pass of the GX method. That is, the fitted return is given by П€^nkОі^kвЉ¤ where П€^nk is the 1Г—k vector of the nth portfolioвЂ™s risk exposures and Оі^k is the 1Г—k vector of the prices of risk of the k latent factors. The left panels show the evidence for the factors extracted using PCA (i.e., RP-PCA with П‰П‰=в€’1вЃ ), whereas the right panels use RP-PCA with П‰ = 20 (i.e., the selected RP-weight). The top panels present the estimates for k = 2, while the bottom panels present the estimates for k = 3. Recall that П†П†(F(1в€’2)) is the optimal SDF for П‰П‰=в€’1вЃ , whereas П†П†(F(1в€’3)) for П‰ = 20. Excess returns are expressed in percent per annum (pa). The sample period runs from November 1983 to December 2017 at a monthly frequency (T = 410).

5.description: Nontradable factorsвЂ™ explained variation by latent pricing factors The figure shows nontradable candidate factorsвЂ™ explained variation by latent factors (extracted using RP-PCA with baseline RP-weight П‰ = 20 applied to the panel of N = 46 currency portfolios). We limit the evidence to the first three extracted factors, given that the optimal SDF is П†П†(F1в€’k) with k = 3 (Figure A4 in the Internet Appendix uses k = 6). Panels A, B, and C present the evidence for financial, text-based and macro candidate factors, respectively. In each panel, candidate factors are sorted by the spanning R2s associated with the k-factor SDF (to help visualize the results, we only display the 30 macro factors with the highest R2s). Bars quantify the latent factor contribution to the overall R2. We consider SDFs of expanding dimension, thus k ranges from 1 to 3. Nontradable factors are expressed as innovations, using the residuals from AR(1) processes, and are then standardized. The sample period varies with the factor at hand, according to data availability over the November 1983 to December 2017 period (T = 410). See factor descriptions in Tables A5вЂ“A7 in the Internet Appendix.

6.description: Return-based candidate factors The figure shows the cumulative returns of selected return-based candidate factors. Factor A is the global economic policy uncertainty index (gepu); factor B is the global FX volatility factor (gvol); factor C is the financial intermediariesвЂ™ capital ratio factor (icap); and factor D is the global FX liquidity factor (gliq). For each candidate factor, we present three versions of return-based factors, by expanding the dimension of the SDF, that is, П†П†(F(1в€’k)) with k = 1, 2, 3. Specifically, for П†П†(F(1в€’k))вЃ , the return-based factor is given by F^1:ktО·^вЉ¤вЃ , where the latent factors are extracted using RP-PCA with П‰ = 20 and the exposures are obtained by estimating the spanning regressions of Equation (15); hence, the underlying exposures are those displayed in the first three columns of TableВ 2 (for icap and gliq, see Table A10 in the Internet Appendix, as their premiums estimates are not statistically significant using the three-factor model). The spanning regression sample period can vary with the factor at hand, according to data availability over the November 1983 to December 2017 period. See the factor descriptions in Tables A5вЂ“A7 in the Internet Appendix.

7.description: Observed versus simulated test-asset return moments The figure shows the moments of the observed and simulated test-asset returns. In Average, we plot the time-series averages of the observed portfolio returns (Observed, in percent annualized) against the means of the time-series averages of the simulated portfolio returns (Simulated, in percent annualized); in Standard deviation, we show the standard deviations of the observed returns against the means of the standard deviations of the simulated returns (in percent, annualized); and in Sharpe ratio, we report the annualized Sharpe ratios of the observed returns against the means of the Sharpe ratios of the simulated returns. The observed cross-sectional standard deviation of the average returns is 0.0173, and the mean of the cross-sectional standard deviation of the simulated average returns is 0.0194. The true data-generating process (DGP) has four factors, and the parameters are calibrated based on the four denoised z-factors (dollar, carry, ST mom, and value). To denoise the z-factors, we use RP-PCA with П‰ = 20 and four pricing factors. We simulate the models 10,000 times with N = 46 and T = 410 (see Section VI and Table A24 in the Internet Appendix).
